The prophet must behave as if he expected his country to be ruined very soon. In anticipation of difficult times, he is to abstain from marriage, mourning for the dead, and pleasure. Those who want to convince others of God’s truths must show by their self-denial that they believe them themselves. Peace, both within and without, in family and public life, is completely the work of God, and comes from his kindness and mercy. When he takes away his peace from a people, distress will follow. There may be times when it is right to avoid things that would otherwise be our duty; and we should not hold tightly to the pleasures and concerns of this life.

This seems to be the language of those who argue with the word of God, and instead of humbling and condemning themselves, they justify themselves, as if God has wronged them. A clear and complete answer is given. They were more stubborn in sin than their fathers, each walking after the desires of his heart. Since they will not listen, they will be driven away into a distant country, a land they do not know. If they had God’s favor, that would make even the land of their captivity pleasant.

The restoration from the Babylonian captivity would be remembered instead of the deliverance from Egypt; it also symbolized spiritual redemption and the future deliverance of the church from anti-Christian oppression. But none of the sins of sinners can be hidden from God, or overlooked by him. He will find out and raise up instruments of his wrath that will destroy the Jews, with deception like fishermen, and with force like hunters. The prophet, rejoicing in the hope of mercy to come, addressed the Lord as his strength and refuge. The deliverance out of captivity will be a symbol of the great salvation to be accomplished by the Messiah. The nations have often known the power of Jehovah in his wrath; but they will know him as the strength of his people, and their refuge in times of trouble.
